Q1.bottleneck: Full table scan (6M rows) with string-based hash aggregation
Q1.root_cause: No early filtering on l_shipdate before aggregation. String keys (l_returnflag, l_linestatus) cause hash overhead. Sequential processing without vectorization. Hash aggregation happens after full scan.

Q3.bottleneck: Full lineitem scan despite selective filters, plus on-the-fly hash index construction
Q3.root_cause: Building hash indexes for customer and orders during query execution (not pre-built). Full lineitem scan (6M rows) without early date filtering. No bloom filter to skip non-matching lineitem rows. Hash probes dominate execution time.

Q6.bottleneck: Sequential predicate evaluation on full table scan
Q6.root_cause: All predicates evaluated in order without branch prediction optimization. No SIMD vectorization. Date range filter (l_shipdate) evaluated first but subsequent predicates not vectorized. Missing predicate reordering based on selectivity.

analysis.overall: GenDB is 2.5-6.2x slower than DuckDB across all queries. Primary issues: (1) lack of vectorized execution, (2) full table scans without early filtering, (3) runtime hash index construction, (4) string-based aggregation overhead, (5) loading unused columns during data ingestion. Q1 has the largest gap (6.2x) due to string hashing and lack of SIMD. Q6 has the best relative performance but still 4.7x slower due to missing vectorization.

recommendations.description: Reorder predicates by selectivity: evaluate most selective predicates first to enable early-out. Order: (1) l_discount BETWEEN (most selective, ~10% rows), (2) l_quantity < 24 (~40%), (3) l_shipdate range (~17% of full dataset). Store intermediate boolean masks to avoid redundant evaluations.

Replace string-based group keys with integer encoding. Pre-compute or inline-convert l_returnflag and l_linestatus to single-byte integers (e.g., 'A'→0, 'N'→1, 'R'→2; 'F'→0, 'O'→1). Use a combined uint16_t key: (returnflag << 8) | linestatus. This eliminates string hashing and string comparison overhead in the hash aggregation.

Skip loading unused columns during data ingestion. For lineitem, skip: l_partkey, l_suppkey, l_linenumber, l_commitdate, l_receiptdate, l_shipinstruct, l_shipmode, l_comment (8 of 16 columns). For customer, skip: c_name, c_address, c_nationkey, c_phone, c_acctbal, c_comment (6 of 8 columns). For orders, skip: o_orderstatus, o_totalprice, o_orderpriority, o_clerk, o_comment (5 of 9 columns). This reduces memory footprint and cache pressure.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Reduce data loading time from 14.2s to ~8-10s. Improve cache locality in queries by 20-30%, translating to 5-10% query speedup across all queries.

recommendations.description: Build hash indexes during data loading phase, not during query execution. Pre-build: (1) customer.c_custkey hash index (150K entries), (2) orders.o_orderkey hash index (1.5M entries), (3) orders.o_custkey hash index (1.5M entries). Store these as persistent index structures. During Q3 execution, filter customer by c_mktsegment using the pre-built c_custkey index, then probe the pre-built o_custkey index.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Eliminate 20-30ms of runtime index construction in Q3 (142ms → 110-120ms)

recommendations.description: Apply l_shipdate filter first to create a bitmap of qualifying rows, then scan only those rows for aggregation. Given that l_shipdate <= '1998-09-02' is highly selective (~98% of SF1 data qualifies), the benefit is moderate but sets up infrastructure for SIMD. Combine with integer group key encoding for maximum effect.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction when combined with integer encoding (marginal alone due to high selectivity)

recommendations.description: Build a bloom filter on the qualified orders.o_orderkey after filtering by customer/orderdate. Use this bloom filter to skip lineitem rows that cannot possibly match during the scan. This avoids expensive hash probes for non-matching rows. Bloom filter size: ~1.5M orders × 10 bits = ~2MB.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 10-20% reduction in Q3 by skipping ~80% of lineitem rows early (142ms → 115-130ms)

recommendations.description: Replace full sort + limit 10 with a min-heap of size 10. Since Q3 has LIMIT 10 and typically produces 100K-500K groups, full sorting is wasteful. Maintain a heap during aggregation or immediately after. This avoids O(N log N) sort in favor of O(N log K) where K=10.

Use SIMD intrinsics (SSE/AVX) to evaluate predicates on multiple rows in parallel. Process 4-8 rows per iteration using vector comparisons for l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity. Accumulate revenue using vector additions. This is a more advanced optimization requiring careful implementation.

storage_changes.change: Skip loading unused columns: l_partkey, l_suppkey, l_linenumber, l_commitdate, l_receiptdate, l_shipinstruct, l_shipmode, l_comment
storage_changes.rationale: These 8 columns are never accessed in the workload. Eliminating them reduces memory usage by ~50% for lineitem table and improves cache locality during scans.

storage_changes.change: Skip loading unused columns: c_name, c_address, c_nationkey, c_phone, c_acctbal, c_comment
storage_changes.rationale: Only c_custkey and c_mktsegment are accessed in Q3. Loading only these 2 columns reduces customer table memory by ~75%.

storage_changes.change: Skip loading unused columns: o_orderstatus, o_totalprice, o_orderpriority, o_clerk, o_comment
storage_changes.rationale: Only o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority are accessed in Q3. Loading only these 4 columns reduces orders table memory by ~55%.

storage_changes.change: Add pre-built hash index on l_orderkey during data loading
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 requires joining lineitem with orders on l_orderkey. Pre-building this hash index (6M entries) during loading avoids runtime construction and enables faster probe-side joins.

storage_changes.change: Add pre-built hash index on c_custkey during data loading
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 joins customer with orders on c_custkey. Pre-building this index (150K entries) eliminates runtime overhead and enables immediate filtering by c_mktsegment.

storage_changes.change: Add two pre-built hash indexes: on o_orderkey and on o_custkey during data loading
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 requires bidirectional joins (customer→orders via o_custkey, orders→lineitem via o_orderkey). Pre-building both indexes (1.5M entries each) enables efficient hash joins without runtime construction.

summary: Iteration 1 identified significant optimization opportunities across all three queries. Top priorities: (1) Integer encoding for Q1 group keys to eliminate string hashing overhead (6.2x gap), (2) Lazy column loading to reduce memory footprint and improve cache locality (affects all queries), (3) Pre-built hash indexes to eliminate Q3 runtime construction overhead (2.5x gap), (4) Predicate reordering for Q6 to enable early filtering (4.7x gap). These changes are low-to-medium risk and can be implemented incrementally. Expected cumulative improvement: Q1: 198ms → 120-140ms (30-40% faster), Q3: 142ms → 90-110ms (23-37% faster), Q6: 33ms → 23-28ms (15-30% faster). More advanced optimizations (SIMD, bloom filters) are deferred to later iterations due to higher implementation complexity.
